tools.name: Loomly
tools.short_description: Social media management platform for planning, approving, and publishing content, now owned by Bending Spoons.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Loomly is a social media management platform that helps marketing teams, agencies, and brands plan, create, approve, and publish content across multiple social channels from a single visual content calendar. It has no corporate relationship to Loom, the video messaging tool, or Loomio, the decision-making software, despite the similarly styled name; all three are separate companies. Loomly was founded in 2015 by husband-and-wife team Thibaud Clement (CEO) and Noemie Clement (CMO), and the company built its headquarters in New York City. The platform grew steadily through the late 2010s and 2020s by focusing on a specific niche within the crowded social media management category: structured approval workflows and content calendars for marketing teams and agencies managing multiple brands or clients, rather than trying to be the broadest possible all-in-one suite. On January 10, 2025, Loomly was acquired by Bending Spoons, the Italian technology holding company known for acquiring and operationally overhauling established digital products; Bending Spoons' portfolio includes other well-known acquisitions such as Evernote, Meetup, WeTransfer-adjacent tools, Vimeo, StreamYard (formerly Hopin), and Eventbrite, reflecting a broader roll-up strategy of buying mature SaaS brands and improving their unit economics. Financial terms of the Loomly deal were not disclosed. Architecturally, Loomly is built around a shared visual calendar where team members draft posts, attach media, and route them through customizable approval workflows before scheduling and auto-publishing to connected social accounts. Supported channels include the major social networks such as Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, Pinterest, TikTok, YouTube, and Google Business Profile. On top of scheduling, Loomly layers post ideas and trending-topic suggestions, AI-assisted caption and copy generation, brand guideline reminders that flag deviations before publishing, a unified social inbox for managing comments and messages across channels, and analytics reporting to track post and channel performance. Core features include a shared content calendar with drag-and-drop scheduling; customizable multi-step approval workflows so managers or clients can review and approve posts before they go live; AI-assisted post creation and caption generation; automated post ideas based on trending topics, RSS feeds, and observances; a unified social inbox for replying to comments and messages from one place; performance analytics across connected channels; brand guideline checks that warn users before publishing off-brand content; and library and asset management for reusable media. Pricing is structured in publicly listed tiers: the Base plan costs 42 dollars a month for 10 social accounts, the Standard plan costs 80 dollars a month for 20 accounts, the Advanced plan costs 175 dollars a month for 35 accounts, and the Premium plan costs 369 dollars a month for 50 accounts, with an Enterprise tier available at custom pricing for 61 or more accounts and unlimited users across every tier. Annual billing carries roughly a 25 percent discount compared with monthly billing, and a 7-day free trial is available on paid plans with no credit card required. Loomly's target audience spans small business owners managing their own social presence, in-house marketing teams coordinating multi-brand calendars, and digital marketing agencies that need client approval workflows and multi-account management in one place. Its primary competitors are Buffer, Hootsuite, Sprout Social, Later, and Sprinklr, along with newer AI-native scheduling tools. Loomly differentiates itself through its approval-workflow-centric design, which makes it particularly popular with agencies and teams that need sign-off before anything publishes, its relatively transparent published pricing compared with some enterprise-only competitors, and its post-idea and brand-guideline features that reduce the manual effort of ideation and brand consistency checks.

tools.name: Tailwind
tools.short_description: Tailwind (tailwindapp.com) is a social media marketing platform that helps creators and small businesses plan, design, and schedule posts for Pinterest, Instagram, and Facebook.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Tailwind, found at tailwindapp.com, is a social media marketing and scheduling platform built specifically for Pinterest and Instagram, with support for Facebook publishing. It is not related to the Tailwind CSS styling framework and not related to Tailscale, the networking company. Tailwind was founded in 2012 by Danny Maloney and Alex Maloney in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, where the company remains headquartered with roughly 51-200 employees. It has been one of Pinterest's longest-standing official developer partners since 2012 and launched its first scheduling product in 2015. Tailwind's tools include a drag-and-drop design studio with Canva integration, SmartSchedule for posting at optimal times, hashtag and keyword research, Pinterest and Instagram analytics, and a Smart.bio link-in-bio page. It targets bloggers, e-commerce sellers, content creators, and small marketing teams who rely heavily on visual social platforms to drive traffic. Pricing is freemium: a Free Forever plan allows five posts per month on one account, while paid plans (Pro, Advanced, and Max) scale up post volume, connected accounts, AI credits, and team seats, starting around $17.99 per month when billed annually. Over one million people have used Tailwind's software to manage their social content calendars.

tools.name: Creatio
tools.short_description: Creatio is a no-code CRM and business process management platform combining sales, marketing, and service automation with built-in AI agents.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Creatio is a no-code CRM and business process management (BPM) platform originally founded in 2002 in Ukraine as Terrasoft, which later rebranded its product line as 'bpm'online' before adopting the unified Creatio name in October 2019. The company is now headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, operates six offices with a presence in roughly 25 countries, employs more than 700 people, and has raised over $278 million in funding from investors including Volition Capital and Sapphire Ventures, reaching unicorn status (a $1.2 billion valuation) in 2024. Creatio's platform sits at the intersection of CRM and no-code process automation: it ships Sales, Marketing, and Service 'Creatio' modules built on top of a shared no-code development studio, so customers can both use out-of-the-box CRM functionality and visually build or customize their own business applications and workflows without writing code. The no-code studio includes a drag-and-drop process designer (BPMN-based), a visual data model builder, and Freedom UI page designers. Creatio.ai layers generative and predictive AI agents across the platform to draft content, score leads, summarize customer interactions, and recommend next-best actions inside sales, marketing, and service workflows. Pricing follows a per-user, per-month model with three core platform tiers: Growth at roughly $25/user/month for small and mid-sized teams starting with core no-code and CRM capabilities; Enterprise at roughly $55/user/month (some sources cite closer to $40-75/user/month depending on packaging) for larger organizations needing broader automation and integration; and Unlimited at roughly $85/user/month for organizations running complex, large-scale automation with no restrictions on use cases. On the Growth and Enterprise tiers, the Sales, Marketing, and Service Creatio modules are sold as separate add-ons at about $15/user/month each, while the Unlimited tier bundles all three modules at no extra per-user charge. Notably, Creatio enforces a minimum purchase commitment of roughly $10,000 per year, which makes it a poor fit for very small teams or solo users despite its per-user pricing structure, positioning it firmly as a mid-market-to-enterprise platform. Creatio is commonly evaluated against Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Pipedrive, and other no-code platforms like Zoho Creator, with its key differentiator being the tight integration between CRM functionality and a full no-code application development environment rather than CRM alone. It targets industries such as financial services, insurance, manufacturing, and professional services that need both standard CRM workflows and the flexibility to model custom, industry-specific business processes without a dedicated engineering team.

tools.name: Monday Sales CRM
tools.short_description: Monday Sales CRM is a customizable, no-code CRM product built on the monday.com Work OS that helps sales teams manage pipelines, contacts, and post-sales workflows.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Monday Sales CRM is a purpose-built customer relationship management product from monday.com Ltd (formerly dapulse Labs), the same company that operates the broader monday.com Work OS platform. monday.com was founded in Tel Aviv in 2012 by Roy Mann and Eran Zinman, renamed from dapulse to monday.com in December 2017, and went public on Nasdaq in June 2021, raising roughly $235 million across its funding history before the IPO. Unlike general project-management boards, Monday Sales CRM is a dedicated, pre-configured template and product line layered on top of monday.com's underlying Work OS architecture, purpose-built for sales teams rather than general work management. It gives sales teams a visual, no-code CRM covering the full deal lifecycle: customizable deal pipelines, contact and account records, lead capture forms, and post-sale account and project management, all built from the same flexible board-and-column engine that powers monday.com's other products. Because it inherits the Work OS foundation, Monday Sales CRM supports the same automation engine, integration marketplace, and dashboard/reporting tools as monday.com, but its templates, fields, and AI features are pre-tuned for sales workflows specifically. Core features include two-way email sync with Gmail and Outlook, email templates and sequences for outreach, mass email sending, AI-powered email generation and lead data enrichment, no-code workflow automations, visual sales dashboards and forecasting, built-in quote and invoice generation, customizable lead capture forms, and over 150 industry-specific templates spanning sales, marketing, and related use cases. Monday Sales CRM is priced on a seat-based subscription model separate from (though similarly structured to) monday.com's Work Management pricing: Basic CRM starts around $12 per seat per month billed annually, Standard around $17 per seat per month, Pro around $28 per seat per month, and Enterprise is custom-quoted; annual billing saves roughly 18% versus monthly, and all paid plans require a minimum of three seats. A 14-day free trial is available on paid plans. Monday Sales CRM's target audience is sales teams and revenue operations functions at small-to-midsize businesses and departments within larger enterprises who want a highly visual, flexible CRM without heavy implementation overhead. Its main competitors include HubSpot CRM, Pipedrive, Salesforce Sales Cloud, and Zoho CRM. Monday Sales CRM differentiates itself through the same visual, drag-and-drop customization and no-code automation that defines the broader monday.com platform, letting sales teams reshape pipelines and fields freely rather than adapting to a rigid, pre-built CRM data model, though this flexibility can mean more setup work than opinionated, sales-only CRMs.

tools.name: Nimble
tools.short_description: Nimble is a relationship-focused CRM for small businesses that unifies contacts, social signals, email, and pipeline management, with deep integration into Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Nimble is a relationship-focused CRM founded in 2009 and headquartered in Santa Monica, California. Its founder and CEO, Jon Ferrara, previously co-founded GoldMine, one of the earliest contact-management CRM products, in 1989, and sold it for a reported 125 million dollars before starting Nimble to bring a similar founder-led, relationship-first philosophy to a modern, socially-connected CRM. Nimble differentiates itself from larger CRMs like Salesforce and HubSpot by emphasizing simplicity and deep integration with the tools small businesses already use daily, particularly Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, through a browser extension called Nimble Prospector that lets users capture contact and company data from any web page, social profile, or inbox. The platform combines contact management, deal and pipeline tracking, email marketing and sequencing, and social signal enrichment (pulling in social media activity and company data to build a fuller picture of each contact) into one system, with AI-powered features for drafting emails and summarizing relationship history. Nimble's core Business plan is priced at 24.90 dollars per seat per month billed annually (or 29.90 dollars per seat billed monthly), and includes 25,000 contact records, 2 GB of storage per seat, Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace integration, the Nimble Prospector browser extension, and 1,000 group messages per license per month, with a 14-day free trial requiring no credit card. Beyond the base plan, Nimble offers a la carte add-ons rather than multiple rigid tiers: email marketing (15 dollars per month company-wide, unlimited sends), web forms and live chat (12 dollars per month per team), data enrichment credits (10 dollars per month for 100 credits), additional contact capacity (10 dollars per month per additional 10,000 contacts), extra storage (10 dollars per month per additional 10 GB), and larger group-messaging packages (15 to 99 dollars per month for 2,000 to 25,000 emails). This modular pricing lets small businesses pay only for the capacity and features they need as they grow. Nimble's target audience is small businesses and solopreneurs, particularly those who already run their business communication through Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace and want a CRM that layers on top of existing email and calendar workflows rather than replacing them. The company is headquartered in Santa Monica with an employee count generally estimated in the low hundreds. Its primary competitors include HubSpot CRM, Zoho CRM, Insightly, Pipedrive, and Salesforce, with Nimble's key differentiators being its social-signal-driven contact enrichment, tight Office 365/Google Workspace integration, and a founder-led company culture inherited from Jon Ferrara's earlier success building GoldMine.
